name: Bagua
shortDescription: The Bagua are eight trigrams used in Taoist cosmology to represent the fundamental principles of reality, seen as a range of eight interrelated concepts.
description: The Bagua (八卦; bā guà) are eight trigrams used in Taoist cosmology. Each trigram consists of three lines, either broken (yin) or unbroken (yang), representing a different combination of these fundamental principles. The Bagua are arranged in various configurations, most notably the Earlier Heaven (先天八卦; xiāntiān bāguà) and Later Heaven (後天八卦; hòutiān bāguà) sequences. The Earlier Heaven arrangement reflects an ideal, primordial state of balance and opposition, while the Later Heaven arrangement represents the dynamic, ever-changing reality of the manifested world. The trigrams are associated with various natural phenomena, family members, directions, and even parts of the body. Their primary function is to provide a framework for understanding the interplay of yin and yang and the constant flux of the universe. They are central to practices such as Feng Shui, divination (I Ching), and martial arts. The Bagua evolved from earlier cosmological diagrams and were further developed during the Zhou Dynasty (1046–256 BCE). They are deeply connected to the concept of the Tao, representing its manifestation in the phenomenal world. The principles of the Bagua are also linked to the five elements (wuxing), further enriching the cosmological framework. The Bagua serve as a tool to understand how to live in harmony with the Tao and to predict future events. They provide a framework for understanding the complex relationships between all things in the universe and their constant transformation.
